name: Ériu
description: Ériu is a goddess in Irish mythology, considered one of the three sovereign goddesses of Ireland, along with her sisters Banba and Fódla. These goddesses were daughters of Ernmas of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Ériu is often seen as the personification of Ireland itself, her name being the source of the modern name 'Ireland' (Éire). She is associated with sovereignty, land, and the power of the land. In some accounts, she is the wife of Mac Grianna, one of the three kings who ruled Ireland before the arrival of the Gaels. When the Milesians (Gaels) arrived, Ériu met them and prophesied that they would rule the land, granting them permission to settle. Her acceptance of the Milesians is crucial to their legitimacy as rulers. She is depicted as a powerful and wise figure, deeply connected to the land and its destiny. Her role in the myths is vital in establishing the divine right of the Gaels to rule Ireland and establishing a connection between the land and its people. She represents the enduring spirit of Ireland and its connection to the divine feminine. Ériu's influence continues to be felt in Irish culture and identity, symbolizing the land's ancient heritage and enduring spirit.
subtitle: Sovereign Goddess of Ireland

keyMyths.title: Meeting the Milesians
keyMyths.description: Ériu meets the Milesians upon their arrival in Ireland and prophesies their rule, granting them permission to settle. This act legitimizes their claim to the land.
keyMyths.source: Lebor na hUidre (Book of the Dun Cow)

keyMyths.title: Sovereignty Goddess
keyMyths.description: Ériu, along with her sisters, represents the sovereignty of Ireland. Her acceptance is necessary for any king to rightfully rule the land.

keyMyths.title: Names of Ireland
keyMyths.description: Ireland has been known by many names, including Ériu, Banba, and Fódla, each representing a different aspect of the land's spirit and history.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz

sources.text: A compilation of early Irish myths and legends.
